sections.title: Overview
sections.text: Mercury is the smallest planet in the Solar System and the closest to the Sun. Its cratered surface closely resembles the Moon, while its high density makes it the second densest planet after Earth.

sections.title: History of Exploration
sections.text: The first detailed images were captured by Mariner 10 in 1974. In 2004, the MESSENGER mission was launched, operating until 2015. In 2018, ESA and JAXA launched BepiColombo, which is scheduled to enter orbit in 2025.

sections.title: Quick Facts
text: Temperatures range from -180°C at night to +430°C during the day
A year lasts only 88 Earth days
A solar day is equal to 176 Earth days

sections.title: Unique Features
sections.text: Due to its proximity to the Sun, Mercury is difficult to study from Earth. Its orbit and rotation create a unique 3:2 spin-orbit resonance: the planet completes three rotations on its axis for every two orbits around the Sun.

marquee: Smallest planet in the system
A day is longer than a year
Extreme temperature fluctuations

sections.title: Overview
sections.text: Venus is the second planet from the Sun and Earth's near-twin in size. Its thick atmosphere traps heat and creates crushing pressure, turning the surface into a hellish desert.

sections.title: Exploration History
sections.text: In 1970, Venera 7 performed the first soft landing. In the 21st century, its atmosphere was studied by ESA's Venus Express and Japan's Akatsuki.

sections.title: Quick Facts
text: Surface temperature reaches 470°C
Atmosphere: 96% CO₂
Pressure: 90x higher than Earth's

sections.title: Key Features
sections.text: Venus rotates retrogradely (backwards). Its brilliance makes it visible as the iconic 'Morning Star' or 'Evening Star'.

marquee: The hottest planet in the system
Ocean-depth atmospheric pressure
Brightest object in the night sky

sections.title: Overview
sections.text: Earth is the third planet from the Sun and the only known world to harbor life. It features vast oceans, a protective atmosphere, and active geology.

sections.title: Exploration History
sections.text: The first space-based images were captured in 1946. Later, the Apollo program famously revealed the planet from afar as the 'Blue Marble'.

sections.title: Quick Facts
text: 71% water-covered surface
Diameter: 12,742 km
Natural satellite: The Moon

sections.title: Key Features
sections.text: A robust magnetic field and atmosphere shield Earth from cosmic radiation, maintaining the delicate conditions necessary for life.

marquee: The only planet with life
Oceans and continents drive climate
Moon-driven tidal cycles

sections.title: Overview
sections.text: Mars is the fourth planet from the Sun, famously known as the Red Planet due to the prevalence of iron oxide. Its thin atmosphere is primarily composed of carbon dioxide.

sections.title: Exploration History
sections.text: Telescopic observations began in the 17th century. Modern exploration was marked by the Viking missions (1975) and Mars Pathfinder (1997), followed by the Spirit, Opportunity, Curiosity, and Perseverance rovers.

sections.title: Quick Facts
text: Home to Olympus Mons, a 21km high volcano
A Martian day (Sol) lasts 24 hours 37 minutes
Polar ice caps consist of water and dry ice

sections.title: Key Features
sections.text: Mars experiences seasons similar to Earth's, though they last twice as long. The presence of sub-surface ice makes it the prime candidate for future colonization.

marquee: The Red Planet with frozen poles
Olympus Mons is thrice the height of Everest
Days nearly identical to Earth's

sections.title: Overview
sections.text: Jupiter is the fifth planet from the Sun and the Solar System's largest gas giant. Its atmosphere consists primarily of hydrogen and helium, with iconic cloud bands driven by massive storms.

sections.title: Exploration History
sections.text: In 1610, Galileo discovered its largest moons. Since then, Jupiter has been explored by the Pioneer and Voyager flybys, the Galileo orbiter, and the ongoing Juno mission.

sections.title: Quick Facts
text: Diameter: approximately 143,000 km
95 moons, with Ganymede being the largest
The Great Red Spot: a storm raging for over 300 years

sections.title: Key Features
sections.text: Jupiter's immense gravity shapes the orbits of asteroids and acts as a cosmic shield, protecting the inner planets from potential impacts.

marquee: The Solar System's largest gas giant
Ganymede is larger than Mercury
A storm that has lasted for centuries

sections.title: Overview
sections.text: Saturn is the sixth planet from the Sun and the second-largest gas giant. It is world-renowned for its extensive ring system, composed of billions of ice and rock particles.

sections.title: Exploration History
sections.text: Telescopic study began in the 17th century. In the late 20th century, Pioneer and Voyager revealed ring details, followed by the Cassini mission (2004–2017), which provided deep insights into the planet and its moons.

sections.title: Quick Facts
text: Diameter: approximately 120,500 km
82 confirmed moons
Rings span hundreds of thousands of kilometers

sections.title: Key Features
sections.text: Its rings make Saturn the most recognizable planet. Its largest moon, Titan, is unique for having a dense, Earth-like atmosphere.

marquee: The Lord of the radiant rings
Titan: A world with its own atmosphere
Visible even through small telescopes

sections.title: Overview
sections.text: Uranus is the seventh planet from the Sun and an ice giant with a unique axial tilt of nearly 98°. This causes the planet to rotate virtually on its side.

sections.title: Exploration History
sections.text: Discovered in 1781 by William Herschel. In 1986, Voyager 2 became the only spacecraft to ever visit and perform a close study of the planet.

sections.title: Quick Facts
text: Axial tilt is nearly parallel to its orbital plane
27 moons and 13 faint rings
Methane in the atmosphere creates its signature turquoise hue

sections.title: Key Features
sections.text: Due to its extreme tilt, seasons last for decades: a single summer or winter can persist for 42 Earth years.

marquee: The planet that rotates on its side
Turquoise color driven by methane
Seasons that last 42 years

sections.title: Overview
sections.text: Neptune is the eighth planet from the Sun, a gas giant distinguished by its deep blue color. Its atmosphere is home to the most powerful winds in the Solar System.

sections.title: Exploration History
sections.text: Discovered in 1846 by Le Verrier and Galle. In 1989, Voyager 2 performed its final flyby, providing the first close-up images of this distant world.

sections.title: Quick Facts
text: Winds reach speeds of up to 2,100 km/h
14 moons, with Triton being the largest
Methane absorption gives the atmosphere its blue hue

sections.title: Key Features
sections.text: Located at the edge of the Solar System, Neptune's gravity significantly influences the movement of objects within the Kuiper Belt.

marquee: The blue sentinel of the outer system
Supersonic winds faster than sound
Triton moves in a retrograde orbit
